title: Malicious Attachment Tracking
dataSources: Email gateway attachment scanning logs, safe attachments logs
spl:
index=email sourcetype="ms:o365:messageTrace"
| search FilteringResult="*malware*" OR FilteringResult="*SafeAttachment*"
| stats count by SenderAddress, Subject, FilteringResult
| sort -count
description: Attachment-based threats bypass URL filtering. Tracking by file type reveals attack vectors and informs policy decisions.
value: Attachment-based threats bypass URL filtering. Tracking by file type reveals attack vectors and informs policy decisions.
implementation: Enable attachment scanning in email gateway. Ingest scanning results. Track detections by file type, sender domain, and verdict. Alert on malicious attachments reaching users (detection after delivery). Report on blocked attachment statistics.
visualization: Bar chart (detections by file type), Table (malicious attachments), Line chart (detection trend), Pie chart (verdict distribution).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Email.All_Email
  where (All_Email.action="*malware*" OR All_Email.signature="*malware*")
  by All_Email.src_user All_Email.recipient All_Email.file_name
| sort -count
knownFalsePositives: Authorized security test attachments (EICAR), user awareness attachments in training modules, and vendor compliance test files that intentionally resemble malware for validation.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Email security TA.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: Email gateway attachment scanning logs, safe attachments logs.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Enable attachment scanning in email gateway. Ingest scanning results. Track detections by file type, sender domain, and verdict. Alert on malicious attachments reaching users (detection after delivery). Report on blocked attachment statistics.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=email sourcetype="ms:o365:messageTrace"
| search FilteringResult="*malware*" OR FilteringResult="*SafeAttachment*"
| stats count by SenderAddress, Subject, FilteringResult
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Malicious Attachment Tracking** ‚Äî Attachment-based threats bypass URL filtering. Tracking by file type reveals attack vectors and informs policy decisions.

Documented **Data sources**: Email gateway attachment scanning logs, safe attachments logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Email security TA. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: email; **sourcetype**: ms:o365:messageTrace. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=email, sourcetype="ms:o365:messageTrace". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by SenderAddress, Subject, FilteringResult** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Email.All_Email
  where (All_Email.action="*malware*" OR All_Email.signature="*malware*")
  by All_Email.src_user All_Email.recipient All_Email.file_name
| sort -count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Malicious Attachment Tracking** ‚Äî Attachment-based threats bypass URL filtering. Tracking by file type reveals attack vectors and informs policy decisions.

Documented **Data sources**: Email gateway attachment scanning logs, safe attachments logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Email security TA. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Email.All_Email` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Compare search results in Splunk with the Microsoft 365 Defender portal (https://security.microsoft.com) and, where applicable, the Proofpoint TAP dashboard, Mimecast Personal Portal, or Cisco ESA `mail_logs`. Confirm events are present, fields parse correctly, and sample ratios match the vendor console for the same time range.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (detections by file type), Table (malicious attachments), Line chart (detection trend), Pie chart (verdict distribution).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for malware and high-risk attachments in the mail path, so dangerous files are blocked or flagged before they reach inboxes and spread inside the org.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM `Email.All_Email` dataset once message and attachment fields are CIM-tagged; otherwise `cimSpl` tstats may return no rows.
